File_Name: ABB Holdings Pte Ltd and Others v Sher Hock Guan Charles.pdf
Issue: Whether the defendant, Sher Hock Guan Charles, breached his express, implied, and fiduciary duties (including duties of good faith, fidelity, non-disclosure, and avoiding conflict of interest) to the plaintiffs (ABB Holdings Pte Ltd, ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd, and ABB Industry Pte Ltd) by engaging with a competitor (Huadian) while still employed by the plaintiffs.
Rule: The legal rules applied include: express contractual duties (e.g., to use best endeavours, avoid conflicts, refrain from competing, maintain secrecy); implied duties (e.g., good faith and fidelity, non-misuse of confidential information, disclosure of threats); and fiduciary duties (e.g., acting in good faith and best interests, avoiding conflict of interest, disclosing relevant information, and informing of damaging activities). Specifically, the court considered the duty of fidelity and fiduciary duties regarding actively assisting a competitor while still employed.
Application: The court found that the defendant likely decided to join Huadian (a competitor) by November 2002, while still employed by the plaintiffs. He was found in breach for: (1) failing to inform the second and third plaintiffs of XIHARI’s intention to develop and market a new generation of circuit breakers; (2) communicating with Mr. Leonhardt with a view to obtaining his services as a consultant for XIHARI and Huadian, thereby actively assisting a competitor; and (3) spending time after about November 2002 working on plans to develop and run the business of Huadian, as evidenced by a detailed business plan presented shortly after his official departure, which the court determined must have been prepared in advance. The court clarified that merely accepting an offer to join a competitor was not a breach, but actively assisting its establishment and operations while still employed was.
Conclusion: Judgment was given for the second and third plaintiffs against the defendant. The defendant was found liable for breach of fiduciary duty and duty of fidelity in the three specific respects outlined in the application. The remedy awarded to the second and third plaintiffs is either damages to be assessed or an account of the defendant’s profits, at their election. The claim of the first plaintiff against the defendant was dismissed.
